rules.response: 🌧 Rainfall tip: Avoid applying fertilizers or pesticides just before rain — they will wash away. Wait 24 hours after rain before spraying. Heavy rainfall? Ensure your field has proper drainage to avoid waterlogging.
☀️ In hot weather: Water your crops early morning (5–8 AM) or evening (6–8 PM) to reduce evaporation. Mulching around plants helps retain soil moisture. Avoid working in fields between 12 PM – 3 PM.

🌨 Cold weather alert: Cover young seedlings with cloth or plastic sheets during frost nights. Irrigate fields before a cold night — wet soil retains heat better. Avoid sowing heat-loving crops like cotton in winter.

🏜 Drought management: Switch to drip irrigation to save up to 50% water. Grow drought-resistant crops like jowar, bajra, or pulses. Mulch your soil to reduce evaporation. Check with your district office for government drought relief schemes.

🌊 Flood/waterlogging: Immediately create drainage channels to remove excess water. Do not apply fertilizers in waterlogged fields. After water recedes, spray fungicide to prevent root rot. Report crop damage to your local agriculture office for compensation.
🌦 For accurate daily weather forecasts for your district, visit: meghdoot.imd.gov.in (India Met Dept) or check the Kisan app. You can also SMS WEATHER district-name to 51969 for a free forecast.

responses: 🌦 For accurate weather info, visit meghdoot.imd.gov.in or use the Kisan app on your phone.
🌧 Good practice: always check weather before spraying pesticides or fertilizers.
☀️ Water your crops early morning or evening — never during peak afternoon heat.

rules.response:
🌾 Paddy/Rice cultivation tips:
• Best season: Kharif (June–November)
• Water requirement: 1200–1500mm
• Sow seeds in nursery first, transplant after 25–30 days
• Apply basal fertilizer (NPK 60:30:30 kg/ha) at transplanting
• Watch for brown planthopper and blast disease
• Harvest when 80% grains turn golden yellow

🌾 Wheat cultivation tips:
• Best season: Rabi (November–March)
• Sow seeds at 100–125 kg/ha depth of 5–6cm
• First irrigation: 20–25 days after sowing (crown root stage)
• Apply nitrogen in 2 splits: at sowing and at first irrigation
• Watch for yellow rust and aphids
• Harvest when crop turns golden and moisture is below 14%

🌿 Cotton cultivation tips:
• Best season: Kharif (April–May sowing)
• Use Bt cotton seeds for bollworm resistance
• Spacing: 90x60cm for irrigated, 90x45cm for rainfed
• Apply potassium to improve fiber quality
• Monitor for pink bollworm and whitefly regularly
• Pick cotton every 8–10 days once bolls open

🍅 Tomato cultivation tips:
• Can be grown year-round; best in Oct–Nov and Jan–Feb
• Prepare nursery, transplant 25–30 day old seedlings
• Spacing: 60x45cm
• Stake plants when they reach 30cm height
• Water regularly but avoid waterlogging
• Watch for early blight, late blight, and fruit borer

🧅 Onion cultivation tips:
• Kharif sowing: June–July | Rabi sowing: Oct–Nov
• Transplant 6–8 week old seedlings
• Avoid excess nitrogen — causes poor bulb formation
• Stop irrigation 10 days before harvest
• Cure bulbs in shade for 7–10 days before storage
• Store in well-ventilated shade to avoid rotting

🥔 Potato cultivation tips:
• Best season: Rabi (Oct–Nov planting)
• Use certified seed tubers, cut into 40–50g pieces
• Plant at 60x20cm spacing, 5–7cm deep
• Earth up plants when 15–20cm tall
• Apply fertilizer NPK 180:80:100 kg/ha
• Harvest when tops turn yellow and die naturally

🌽 Maize cultivation tips:
• Kharif: June–July | Rabi: Oct–Nov
• Seed rate: 20–25 kg/ha at 60x20cm spacing
• Highly responsive to nitrogen — apply in 3 splits
• Needs good drainage — avoid waterlogged fields
• Watch for fall armyworm — a major pest recently
• Harvest when husks turn brown and kernels are hard

🫘 Soybean cultivation tips:
• Best season: Kharif (June–July)
• Seed rate: 70–80 kg/ha at 45x5cm spacing
• Treat seeds with Rhizobium culture before sowing
• Do not apply nitrogen fertilizer — soybean fixes its own
• Watch for girdle beetle and leaf eating caterpillars
• Harvest when 95% pods turn brown

☔ Kharif season crops (June–October):
• Cereals: Paddy, Maize, Bajra, Jowar
• Oilseeds: Groundnut, Soybean, Sunflower
• Pulses: Moong, Urad, Arhar (Tur)
• Cash crops: Cotton, Sugarcane, Jute
Sow after first good monsoon rain when soil has good moisture.

❄️ Rabi season crops (November–March):
• Cereals: Wheat, Barley
• Oilseeds: Mustard, Linseed, Sunflower
• Pulses: Chickpea, Peas, Lentil
• Vegetables: Potato, Onion, Tomato, Cauliflower
Sow after monsoon retreat when temperatures drop below 25°C.

🪴 Soil health tips:
• Test soil every 2–3 years at your nearest KVK or soil testing lab
• Ideal pH for most crops: 6.0–7.5
• Add farm yard manure (FYM) 10–15 tonnes/ha to improve organic matter
• Practice crop rotation to maintain fertility
• Avoid burning crop residues — incorporate them into soil instead
• Apply lime if soil is too acidic (pH below 5.5)

🌱 Seed selection tips:
• Always buy certified seeds from government outlets or reputed companies
• Check for germination date — seeds older than 1 year may have poor germination
• Treat seeds with fungicide (Thiram/Captan) before sowing
• For Telangana: contact TSSEED or local agriculture department for state-recommended varieties
• Hybrid seeds give higher yield but must be purchased every season

responses: 🌱 For Kharif season (June–Oct): Paddy, Maize, Cotton, Soybean are ideal crops for Telangana.
🌾 For Rabi season (Nov–Mar): Wheat, Mustard, Chickpea work well in most regions.
🪴 Always test your soil before deciding which crop to grow. Visit your nearest KVK for a free soil test.
💡 Rotate crops every season to maintain soil health and reduce pest buildup.

🏛 MSP (Minimum Support Price) 2024–25 — Full List:

🌾 Cereals:
• Paddy (Common): ₹2,183/qtl
• Wheat: ₹2,275/qtl
• Maize: ₹2,090/qtl
• Bajra: ₹2,625/qtl
• Jowar (Hybrid): ₹3,371/qtl
• Barley: ₹1,735/qtl

🫘 Pulses:
• Arhar/Tur: ₹7,550/qtl
• Moong: ₹8,682/qtl
• Urad: ₹7,400/qtl
• Chickpea: ₹5,440/qtl
• Lentil: ₹6,425/qtl

🌻 Oilseeds:
• Groundnut: ₹6,783/qtl
• Mustard: ₹5,950/qtl
• Soybean: ₹4,892/qtl
• Sunflower: ₹7,280/qtl

🌿 Cash Crops:
• Cotton (Long): ₹7,521/qtl
• Sugarcane: ₹340/qtl

MSP is fixed by CACP. Always sell at or above MSP.

📊 How to check live daily mandi prices:

🌐 Website: agmarknet.gov.in
📱 App: Kisan Suvidha (free on Android)
📱 App: e-NAM (National Agriculture Market)
📱 App: Fasal (provides crop price alerts)
📲 SMS: Send crop name to 51969
☎️ Helpline: 1800-180-1551 (free)

💡 You can also ask me directly:
'Today price for wheat' or 'Mandi price for tomato'
and I will fetch the latest price for you!

🚜 Best ways to sell your produce:

1. 🏪 Local APMC Mandi — most common, transparent auction
2. 💻 e-NAM Portal (enam.gov.in) — online national market
3. 🤝 FPO (Farmer Producer Organisation) — collective bargaining
4. 🏭 Direct to processor/company — contract farming, stable price
5. 🛒 Direct to consumer — weekly markets, online platforms
6. 🏛 Government procurement (FCI/NAFED/NCCF) — MSP guaranteed

💡 Joining an FPO typically gives 10–20% better prices than selling alone.

🏪 Crop Storage Tips for Better Prices:

• Warehouse Receipt System: Store at WDRA-registered warehouses and get loans against stored produce
• Cold storage: Potato, onion, tomato — rent ₹150–200/qtl/month but price doubles
• Grain storage: Keep below 12–14% moisture, use hermetic bags to prevent pest damage
• Government scheme: PM-AASHA supports price deficiency payments
• Never sell in distress — wait 4–6 weeks post-harvest for prices to recover

🤝 Farmer Producer Organisation (FPO) Benefits:

• Collective selling means more bargaining power — 10–20% better prices
• Direct access to bulk buyers, exporters, and processors
• Input procurement at bulk discounts (seeds, fertilizers, pesticides)
• Access to government schemes and bank credit more easily
• NABARD supports FPO formation — contact your district agriculture office
• e-NAM platform allows FPOs to sell directly to buyers across India

🌍 Export Opportunities for Indian Farmers:

• Onion: Major export to Malaysia, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, UAE
• Chilli: Large demand from USA, UK, Germany, Japan
• Turmeric: USA and Japan pay premium for organic turmeric
• Basmati Rice: Middle East and Europe are large buyers
• Groundnut: Export to China and Southeast Asia

📋 How to access export prices:
• APEDA (apeda.gov.in) — Agricultural Export Development
• Register your FPO with APEDA for direct export access
• Organic certification increases export price by 30–50%

responses:

💡 Store your produce if prices are low at harvest — they usually recover within 4–8 weeks. Use the Warehouse Receipt System for loans against stored produce.

rules.response:
🍂 Yellow leaves can indicate several issues:

1. Nitrogen deficiency — apply urea (46% N) at 20kg/acre
2. Waterlogging — improve drainage immediately
3. Iron deficiency — spray ferrous sulphate 0.5%
4. Yellow mosaic virus — remove and destroy infected plants
5. Root rot fungus — apply Trichoderma to soil

Check: is yellowing starting from older leaves (bottom) = nutrient deficiency. Starting from new leaves (top) = may be viral/fungal.

🥀 Wilting crops — possible causes:

1. Fusarium wilt (fungal) — soil-borne, no cure once infected. Remove plant. Treat soil with Carbendazim.
2. Water stress — check soil moisture, irrigate if dry
3. Root knot nematode — apply Carbofuran granules to soil
4. Bacterial wilt — infected plants ooze milky liquid when stem cut. Remove and destroy.

Prevention: Use resistant varieties, treat seeds before sowing.

🐜 Aphid control:

• Organic: Spray neem oil (5ml/litre) + few drops of soap
• Chemical: Imidacloprid 0.5ml/litre or Dimethoate 2ml/litre
• Spray in early morning or evening — never in afternoon heat
• Natural predators: ladybird beetles eat aphids — avoid broad-spectrum pesticides
• Yellow sticky traps help monitor aphid levels
• Repeat spray after 7–10 days if infestation continues

🦟 Whitefly control (major virus vector):

• Remove heavily infested leaves and destroy them
• Yellow sticky traps at 10/acre help trap adults
• Spray: Thiamethoxam 0.3g/litre or Spiromesifen 1ml/litre
• Alternate sprays to prevent resistance
• Whiteflies spread leaf curl virus — act early!
• Avoid planting cotton near tomato/chilli — cross-infestation risk

🐛 Borer management:

• For stem borer: Apply Chlorpyriphos 2.5ml/litre at base of plant
• For fruit borer: Spray Spinosad 0.5ml/litre or Emamectin 0.5g/litre
• Remove and destroy infested stems/fruits
• Pheromone traps (5/acre) to monitor and trap adult moths
• For paddy stem borer: Cartap hydrochloride granules in leaf whorl
• Intercrop with coriander or marigold to repel borers naturally

🍄 Fungal disease management:

• Early blight/Late blight: Spray Mancozeb 2.5g/litre or Chlorothalonil 2g/litre
• Powdery mildew (white powder on leaves): Spray Sulphur 3g/litre
• Downy mildew: Spray Metalaxyl + Mancozeb 2.5g/litre
• Rust (orange/red pustules): Spray Propiconazole 1ml/litre
• Root rot: Drench soil with Trichoderma viride 5g/litre

Always spray in the evening. Repeat every 10–14 days.

🌿 Organic/natural pest control methods:

• Neem oil spray: 5ml neem oil + 2ml soap/litre water — effective against most soft insects
• Neem seed kernel extract (NSKE) 5% — very effective, cheap
• Garlic-chilli spray: Blend 100g garlic + 10 chillies in 1L water, dilute 1:10
• Sticky yellow traps for whitefly, aphids, thrips
• Trichoderma (bio-fungicide) for soil fungal diseases
• Beauveria bassiana spray for caterpillars and beetles
• Intercropping with marigold repels nematodes

💦 Spraying best practices:

• Best time: Early morning (6–9 AM) or evening (5–7 PM)
• Never spray in strong wind or before expected rain
• Wear gloves, mask, and full-sleeved clothes while spraying
• Do not eat, drink, or smoke while spraying
• Clean sprayer thoroughly after use
• Alternate chemicals each spray to prevent pest resistance
• Observe waiting period before harvesting after spray
• Keep children and animals away from sprayed area for 24 hours

responses:
🐛 To help you better, can you describe the symptoms?

• Are leaves turning yellow or brown?
• Are there spots, holes, or powder on leaves?
• Is the plant wilting or drooping?
• Are there visible insects on the plant?

Describe what you see and I will give specific advice!

🌿 Common pest issues I can help with:

1. Yellow/brown leaves → nutrient or fungal issue
2. Wilting plant → wilt disease or water stress
3. Holes in leaves → caterpillar or beetle attack
4. Sticky leaves → aphid infestation
5. White powder on leaves → powdery mildew

Which symptom matches your crop?

⚠️ For accurate pest diagnosis always check:

• Which part is affected — leaves, stem, root, or fruit?
• When did symptoms first appear?
• What crop is it and how old is it?

Share these details for better advice!

wizard.start:
🌱 Let me help you find the best crop!

Question 1 of 3:
💧 What is your water availability?

Reply with:
1 — Good irrigation (bore well / canal)
2 — Only rainwater (no irrigation)
3 — Limited water (occasional irrigation)
wizard.q2:
Question 2 of 3:
🗓 Which season are you planting in?

Reply with:
1 — Kharif (June – October, monsoon)
2 — Rabi (November – March, winter)
3 — Summer (March – May)
wizard.q3:
Question 3 of 3:
🎯 What is your main goal?

Reply with:
1 — Maximum profit (cash crop)
2 — Food for family + some selling
3 — Safe crop with less risk

recommendations.limited_kharif_2:
🏆 Best crops for you:

1. 🌾 Jowar — Excellent with limited water
2. 🫘 Moong — Very short duration
3. 🌾 Bajra — Extremely water efficient

💡 Top pick: Jowar — perfect limited water food crop.
recommendations.limited_kharif_3:
🏆 Best crops for you:

1. 🌾 Bajra — Most water efficient crop
2. 🫘 Moong — Safe, short 60 day crop
3. 🌾 Jowar — Reliable with minimal water

💡 Top pick: Bajra — safest crop for limited water.
recommendations.limited_rabi_1:
🏆 Best crops for you:

1. 🌿 Mustard — Excellent with limited water
2. 🫘 Chickpea — Low water requirement
3. 🌾 Barley — Most water efficient Rabi crop

💡 Top pick: Mustard — best profit with limited Rabi water.
recommendations.limited_rabi_2:
🏆 Best crops for you:

1. 🫘 Chickpea — Family protein needs
2. 🌿 Mustard — For cooking oil
3. 🌾 Barley — Animal feed + family use

💡 Top pick: Chickpea + Mustard for balanced family needs.
recommendations.limited_rabi_3:
🏆 Best crops for you:

1. 🫘 Chickpea — Safest limited water Rabi crop
2. 🌿 Mustard — Very low risk
3. 🌾 Barley — Hardy and reliable

💡 Top pick: Chickpea — lowest risk limited water crop.
recommendations.limited_summer_1:
🏆 Best crops for you:

1. 🌻 Sunflower — Manages with limited irrigation
2. 🌽 Maize — Can manage with 3–4 irrigations
3. 🥒 Okra — Short duration, low water

💡 Top pick: Sunflower — best summer profit with limited water.
recommendations.limited_summer_2:
🏆 Best crops for you:

1. 🥒 Okra (Bhindi) — Family vegetable + selling
2. 🌻 Sunflower — Oilseed for family use
3. 🌽 Maize — Cattle feed + family food

💡 Top pick: Okra — great family + income crop.
recommendations.limited_summer_3:
🏆 Best crops for you:

1. 🌻 Sunflower — Very safe summer crop
2. 🥒 Okra — Short duration, easy to grow
3. 🌽 Maize — Reliable with minimal water

💡 Top pick: Sunflower — safest limited water summer crop.
